title: CS50 2D - Lecture 5 - Legend of Zelda
summary: CS50 2D Lecture 5 covers recreation of Legend of Zelda game mechanics including dungeon generation, hitbox design, screen scrolling, and data-driven game development. The lecture is part of Harvard University's introduction to computer science course available through edX, Harvard Extension School, and OpenCourseWare.
writeup: CS 52D Lecture 5 (GD50) walks through building a top-down 2D dungeon crawler in the style of the original Legend of Zelda, progressing from a bare player-movement prototype through procedural dungeon generation, enemy AI, and screen-scrolling room transitions. The core argument is that a top-down game reuses nearly all the same primitives as a side-scroller (AABB collision, state machines, tweens) but requires a critical new distinction: separating hitboxes (the rectangle that receives damage) from hurtboxes (the rectangle that inflicts it, such as a swung sword). The lecture closes by introducing data-driven design, moving entity definitions into external files so a designer can tune content without touching engine code.
key_points: In a top-down game the player has full freedom on both X and Y axes with no gravity constraint, unlike a side-scrolling platformer where the Y axis is governed by gravity and jumping; this single change ripples through movement, camera, and collision handling.

Hitboxes and hurtboxes are semantically distinct rectangles: a hitbox is the collider that can receive damage (the character body), while a hurtbox is the collider that deals damage (a swung sword), and the engine must treat them differently to avoid a character hurting itself.

Dungeon generation produces a grid of rooms connected by doorways, each room a discrete screen; a switch game object in each room triggers the doors to open, and pressing M reveals a minimap with the current room in red and visited rooms in green.

Screen scrolling in the Zelda style is a tween-based camera pan triggered when the player reaches a doorway edge, not continuous scrolling; this is the same interpolation technique used in prior lectures, applied to the camera rather than a sprite.

Sprite sheets for a sword-wielding character use two distinct atlases at different frame sizes (16x16 for walking, 32x32 for sword animations) because the sword extends the character bounding box, requiring separate hurtbox offsets.

Data-driven design externalizes entity definitions to separate files rather than hard-coding them in engine source, enabling a game designer to add or tune entities independently of the programmer writing collision and rendering systems.

chapters.title: Day Zero: Top-Down Movement in Code
